title-group.article-title: The role of copper and cuproptosis in digestive system cancers: novel therapeutic strategies and mechanistic insights

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Recent research has highlighted the critical role of copper metabolism and a novel form of copper-dependent cell death, termed cuproptosis, in the progression and treatment of these cancers. Copper, an essential trace element, plays a crucial role in various cellular processes, including mitochondrial function, antioxidant defense, and angiogenesis. However, the dysregulation of copper homeostasis in cancer cells can exacerbate genomic instability, promote angiogenesis, and reshape the immune microenvironment, thereby driving tumor progression. This review systematically explores the mechanisms of copper homeostasis and cuproptosis in digestive system cancers. We also explore innovative strategies for overcoming drug resistance and reshaping immune responses through copper chelators, copper ionophores, and nanomedicines that induce cuproptosis. By integrating basic research with clinical evidence, this review aims to provide a scientific perspective on understanding the biological significance of cuproptosis and developing new anticancer strategies, while also stimulating in-depth thinking on the intersection of metabolic intervention and cell death.

institution-wrap.institution: Key research project of the Scientific Research Fund of Aerospace Center Hospital

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Digestive system cancers are among the most significant threats to human health worldwide. According to statistics from the World Health Organization, in 2022, newly diagnosed cases of digestive system cancers accounted for approximately 30% of all malignant tumors globally, with mortality rates remaining alarmingly high.1 The 5-y survival rate for some cancer types, such as pancreatic and liver cancers, is less than 15%.1 These cancers are characterized by their aggressive nature, difficulty in early diagnosis, and propensity for metastasis and recurrence. Additionally, the limitations of current treatment options make digestive system cancers a major challenge in clinical medicine that urgently needs to be addressed. Current treatments for digestive system cancers primarily include surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, targeted therapies, and immunotherapy. However, these treatments commonly face issues such as drug resistance, significant toxic side effects, and substantial individual differences in therapeutic efficacy. Moreover, the complexity of the tumor microenvironment and the characteristics of metabolic reprogramming often lead to the failure of traditional therapies, making it difficult to significantly improve patient survival. In this context, non-canonical vulnerabilities such as metal-ion homeostasis and regulated cell-death programs have emerged as attractive therapeutic entry points.

In recent years, the association between abnormal metal ion metabolism and cancer development has garnered significant attention, with copper ions being particularly highlighted. As an essential trace element in the human body, copper participates in cell proliferation and death through the regulation of cellular metabolism, oxidative stress, mitochondrial respiration, and angiogenesis. However, the abnormal uptake and metabolism of copper by tumor cells can exacerbate genomic instability, promote angiogenesis, and reshape the immune microenvironment, thereby driving malignant progression. More notably, in 2022, scientists discovered a novel copper-dependent form of programmed cell death known as cuproptosis.2 This mechanism, which is distinct from apoptosis, necrosis, or ferroptosis, involves the direct binding of copper ions to mitochondrial lipoyltransferases, leading to enzyme aggregation and a loss of iron‒sulfur proteins.2 This ultimately results in mitochondrial metabolic collapse and cell death. This discovery not only reveals the “double-edged sword” nature of copper ions in cancer (both promoting and inhibiting cancer), but also provides a theoretical basis for precision therapies targeting copper metabolism.

This review systematically examines the mechanisms of copper ion metabolism and cuproptosis in digestive system cancers and their therapeutic potential. It then focuses on the core pathways of cuproptosis and their specific regulatory patterns in digestive system cancers such as gastric, colorectal, and liver cancers. Furthermore, this review explores innovative strategies for overcoming drug resistance and reshaping immune responses through copper chelators, copper ionophores, and nanomedicines that induce cuproptosis. Finally, it looks ahead to the challenges and opportunities in the clinical translation of multidimensional combination therapies based on copper metabolism regulation. By integrating basic research with clinical evidence, this review aims to provide a scientific perspective on understanding the biological significance of cuproptosis and developing new anticancer strategies, while also stimulating in-depth thinking on the intersection of metabolic intervention and cell death.

sec.title: Copper homeostasis in the human body
sec.p: Copper is an important nutrient of about 100–200 mg in the normal human body.3 People mainly obtain copper from food, out of which organ meats and shellfish tend to be the richest food sources of copper. The current recommended daily intake of copper for adults is 0.8–2.4 mg/d to maintain systemic copper homeostasis.4 Copper absorption predominantly occurs in the small intestine, particularly in the duodenum and jejunum. It is then transported to the liver via portal blood, incorporated into ceruloplasmin, and subsequently distributed to tissues throughout the body via the bloodstream5 (Figure 1).

caption.p: Copper absorption and transport in the human body. Dietary copper is absorbed by enterocytes in the small intestine. After absorption, copper is transported via the bloodstream to the liver. In the liver, hepatocytes store a portion of the copper to maintain copper homeostasis. The excess copper is excreted into the bile, continues to travel down the digestive tract, and is ultimately excreted from the body through feces.

sec.p: Copper exists in two oxidation states in biological systems: monovalent copper (Cu+) and divalent copper ions (Cu2+). Dietary Cu2+ cannot be directly absorbed by enterocytes until it is reduced to Cu+ by cell-surface metalloreductases belonging to the six-transmembrane epithelial antigen of the prostate (STEAP) family.6 Copper transporter 1 (CTR1/SLC31A1) is predominantly located in the apical membrane of intestinal epithelial cells within the duodenum and proximal jejunum, where copper absorption primarily occurs.7 CTR1 is a transmembrane protein that forms a homotrimeric structure, with three identical subunits. This arrangement creates a channel through which copper ions can pass. Divalent Metal Transporter 1 (DMT1) is primarily responsible for the absorption of divalent metal ions, such as iron (Fe2+), manganese (Mn2+), and copper (Cu2+).8 However, the absorption of Cu2+ by DMT1 is minimal.8 Studies have shown that the absorption of copper by DMT1 is a compensatory mechanism resulting from the deficiency of SLC31A1.9 Intracellular transport is facilitated by specific copper chaperones. Copper ions can be carried to the trans-Golgi network (TGN) by the antioxidant 1 copper chaperone (ATOX1). The ATPase copper transporting alpha (ATP7A) is located in the trans-Golgi network (TGN) and is a key protein that facilitates the export of copper ions from the cell. In response to increased contents of copper, ATP7A translocates to the plasma membrane or intracellular vesicular compartments from the TGN and then fuses with the plasma membrane of intestinal epithelial cells to export Cu+ into the bloodstream. Free copper ions (Cu2+ or Cu+) are highly reactive and can generate free radicals, leading to oxidative stress. Therefore, transporting copper bound to proteins like ceruloplasmin (CP) and human serum albumin (HSA) prevents potential toxicity and maintains stability in circulation.10 Ceruloplasmin is the primary copper carrier in plasma, binding approximately 90% of copper ions. In addition to ceruloplasmin, a portion of copper ions (about 5%–10%) binds to plasma albumin. A tiny fraction of copper ions (less than 1%) may form complexes with specific amino acids or other small molecules, such as histidine. Copper-bound ceruloplasmin and albumin travel through the bloodstream to the liver via the portal vein. Hepatocytes express specific receptors and transporters that facilitate the uptake of copper. The binding of ceruloplasmin to hepatocyte receptors triggers endocytosis, allowing the uptake of the ceruloplasmin-copper complex into liver cells.11 Hepatocytes can store excess copper in the form of metallothionein or incorporate it into copper-dependent enzymes. This regulation is crucial for maintaining systemic copper homeostasis. If copper levels are still elevated, hepatocytes can export excess copper into the bile via the ATPase copper transporting beta (ATP7B), another copper-transporting ATPase. This excretion helps prevent copper toxicity in the body. The excess copper ions continue to travel down the digestive tract and are eventually excreted through feces (Figure 2).

caption.p: Copper metabolism in the cells. Copper enters the cell mainly in the reduced form (Cu+) via the high-affinity copper transporter SLC31A1 (also known as CTR1), or in the oxidized form (Cu2+) through SLC11A2 after reduction by STEAP metalloreductases. Once inside the cytosol, Cu+ is bound by GSH or MT for buffering and delivery. Copper chaperones, including ATOX1, CCS, and COX17, facilitate targeted copper trafficking to various organelles. ATOX1 directs copper to ATP7B in the Golgi for enzyme loading or export, and may also participate in gene regulation. CCS delivers copper to SOD1 for the conversion of superoxide radicals into hydrogen peroxide, while also participating in transcriptional regulation. COX17 delivers copper to mitochondria, where SCO1 and SCO2 assist in cytochrome c oxidase assembly for OXPHOS activation. Excess copper is exported from the cell through a secretory vehicle formed by the Golgi apparatus and ATP7B.

sec.title: Copper metabolism in the cells
sec.p: After being transported into the cell by CTR1, copper ions form complexes with chaperone proteins and are transported to various cellular compartments, where they regulate distinct cellular processes. In the cytoplasm, glutathione (GSH) and metallothionein (MT) serve as natural intracellular copper ion chelators.12 GSH, a vital intracellular antioxidant, binds to copper ions to form GSH-copper complexes, facilitating the detoxification of free copper and protecting cells from oxidative damage.13 Meanwhile, MT, a metal-binding protein rich in cysteine, has a high affinity for copper, sequestering excess ions to prevent toxicity while allowing for their regulated release when needed by copper-dependent enzymes.14 Together, GSH and MT maintain the dynamic balance of copper within cells, which is crucial for cellular health. Copper chaperones are specialized proteins that facilitate the transport and delivery of copper ions to specific cellular targets. For instance, copper ions bind with copper chaperone proteins such as ATOX1, copper chaperone for superoxide dismutase 1 (CCS), and cytochrome c oxidase copper chaperone (COX17), and are then precisely guided to specific locations within the cell. This sophisticated system ensures that copper ions are effectively utilized in essential biological functions while preventing potential toxicity, highlighting the key role of copper chaperone proteins in cellular copper metabolism (Figure 2).

ATOX1 binds Cu+ through two cysteine residues and is responsible for transporting copper ions from the cytoplasm to the TGN. Here, ATP7B regulates the incorporation of copper into various copper-dependent enzymes and manages excess copper by mediating its excretion from the cell. Upon receiving sufficient copper, ATP7B undergoes a conformational change that enables it to transport copper ions across the Golgi membrane and release them into vesicles for further excretion, thereby maintaining cellular copper metabolism.15

CCS directly interacted with and transported copper ions to superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1). Copper ions are essential for the correct folding and maintenance of the three-dimensional structure of SOD1. Additionally, copper ions participate in the formation of disulfide bonds within SOD1, which is essential for its stability and functional maintenance. SOD1 catalyzes the dismutation of superoxide anion radicals (O2−) into oxygen (O2) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Copper ions serve as electron transfer mediators at the active site of SOD1, allowing it to accept and donate electrons, thereby facilitating the dismutation reaction of superoxide anions.16 SOD1 plays a critical role in maintaining intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) homeostasis, and the inactivation of reactive oxygen species can lead to cell death.

COX17 carries copper ions from the cytoplasm into the intermembrane space of mitochondria.17 However, the specific mechanism by which copper ions penetrate the mitochondrial outer membrane is still not completely understood. In the intermembrane space, COX17 transfers copper to two copper chaperones: SCO1 and COX11.18 SCO1 cooperates with SCO2 and COA6 to insert Cu+ into the CuA site of COX2, which consists of two copper ions.19 COX17, facilitated by COX19 and COA4, transfers Cu+ to COX11, which inserts this copper into the COX1 subunit of cytochrome c oxidase.20 This copper ion insertion is essential for the proper assembly and function of cytochrome c oxidase (COX), which serves as the terminal complex of the electron transport chain (ETC) (Figure 3). The efficient incorporation of copper is vital for maintaining the functionality of the ETC and oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS). Thus, proper copper metabolism is crucial, as it directly influences cellular energy production and overall mitochondrial health, highlighting the importance of copper metabolism in maintaining cellular function.

caption.p: Mitochondrial copper delivery and its role in cytochrome c oxidase assembly and oxidative phosphorylation. Copper is essential for the assembly and function of mitochondrial complex IV. For the CuA site in COX2, copper is transferred sequentially via COX17, SCO1, SCO2, and COA6. For the CuB site in COX1, copper is delivered via COX17 and COX11. These copper centers are required for electron transfer and proton pumping by COX, supporting oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) and ATP production.

sec.title: Copper-induced cell death and cuproptosis
sec.p: Excess copper accumulation in cells can lead to oxidative stress, which plays a central role in mediating various forms of cell death, including apoptosis, necrosis, pyroptosis, and autophagic cell death. While each pathway has distinct characteristics, they all share the common feature of being driven by copper-induced ROS, which disrupts cellular function and leads to irreversible damage. Cuproptosis represents a distinct form of programmed cell death that is fundamentally different from the general cell death associated with copper ion accumulation. While both processes are initiated by elevated copper levels, cuproptosis is characterized by the aggregation of lipoylated mitochondrial enzymes and a loss of Fe–S proteins.2 In contrast, cell death induced by copper accumulation may involve a broader range of mechanisms, including apoptotic and necrotic pathways, depending on the extent of copper toxicity and the cellular context. Understanding the nuances between these two forms of cell death is crucial for elucidating the complex role of copper in cellular health and disease, as well as for developing targeted therapeutic strategies that leverage the distinct mechanisms of cuproptosis.

sec.title: Copper-induced oxidative stress
sec.p: Copper can induce oxidative stress by two mechanisms. First, it can directly catalyze the formation of ROS via a Fenton-like reaction. Second, exposure to elevated levels of copper significantly decreases glutathione levels.

In Fenton-like reactions, copper ions undergo redox cycling between their cuprous (Cu+) and cupric (Cu2+) states. The cuprous ion (Cu+) reduces H2O2, leading to the formation of hydroxyl radicals. These radicals are extremely reactive and can initiate a cascade of damaging reactions within the cell. In addition to producing hydroxyl radicals, copper can also react with molecular oxygen (O2) to generate superoxide (O2•−), another form of ROS21 (Figure 4). Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide (O2•−) are both highly reactive forms of ROS that contribute to oxidative stress through their damaging interactions with cellular components. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) can initiate lipid peroxidation, a process where the radicals react with polyunsaturated fatty acids in cell membranes.22 This leads to the formation of lipid peroxides, which can disrupt membrane integrity and fluidity, ultimately resulting in cell lysis or apoptosis. Additionally, hydroxyl radicals can oxidize amino acid side chains, leading to the alteration of protein structure and function.23 This can result in the loss of enzymatic activity, protein misfolding, and aggregation, affecting critical cellular processes. Furthermore, hydroxyl radicals can induce various types of DNA damage, including single-strand breaks, base modifications, and cross-linking.24 Such damage can interfere with DNA replication and transcription, potentially leading to mutations or cell cycle arrest (Figure 4). Superoxide (O2•−) is also a key contributor to oxidative stress. It can react with itself or other molecules to form hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which, while less reactive than hydroxyl radicals, can still hydroxyl radicals via Fenton reactions. Superoxide also disrupts cellular signaling by interfering with redox pathways, which can alter the activity of signaling proteins and transcription factors. The combined effects of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals can create chain reactions that amplify oxidative damage, ultimately impairing cellular functions and leading to cell death25 (Figure 4).

caption.p: ROS generation and cellular damage are mediated by Cu+. Cu+ reacts with H2O2 via Fenton-like chemistry to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and can also promote superoxide (O2−) formation.

sec.p: GSH is a key antioxidant in the cell, composed of three amino acids: glutamine, cysteine, and glycine. It plays a crucial role in neutralizing ROS and maintaining the cellular redox balance. Excess copper generates ROS, thereby increasing the demand for glutathione and leading to the depletion of antioxidant defenses against oxidative stress. Additionally, copper can also react with glutathione to form copper‒glutathione complexes.26 This conjugation reduces the available free glutathione in the cell, further lowering its levels. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for developing therapeutic strategies aimed at mitigating copper-induced oxidative damage and restoring redox homeostasis, which could potentially offer new insights into treating diseases associated with copper dysregulation, including neurodegenerative diseases and cancer.

sec.title: Mechanism of copper-induced cell death
sec.p: Apoptosis, or programmed cell death, can be triggered by oxidative stress induced by excessive copper. This process typically involves mitochondrial dysfunction, which plays a central role in initiating apoptosis. Elevated copper levels can damage the mitochondrial membrane, leading to the release of cytochrome c, which activates caspases (specifically caspase-9 and caspase-3) to execute cell death.27 Additionally, copper ions may interfere with cellular signaling pathways, activating pro-apoptotic proteins like p53, Bax, and Bak (Figure 5).

caption.p: Cu2+/Cu+ driven oxidative stress and regulated cell death. Copper ions (Cu2+/Cu+) promote excessive ROS generation, triggering multiple forms of regulated cell death. In apoptosis, ROS induce mitochondrial cytochrome c release and caspase activation. In necrosis, ROS damage organelles and cellular membranes. In pyroptosis, ROS activate the NLRP3 inflammasome and caspase-1, leading to IL-1β and IL-18 release. In autophagic cell death, although the AMPK-mTOR-ULK1 axis is activated by copper ions to promote autophagy, ROS block autophagic flux, resulting in impaired autophagosome clearance.

sec.p: Although necrosis has typically been viewed as a form of cell death that occurs incidentally due to severe cellular injury, it can also be deliberately activated under conditions of excessive oxidative stress induced by copper ion accumulation.28 In the case of copper-induced necrosis, high copper concentrations overwhelm the cell's antioxidant defenses and disrupt vital cellular structures, including the plasma membrane, mitochondria, and organelles.29 The cell cannot maintain homeostasis, leading to irreversible damage and rupture (Figure 5).

Pyroptosis is a form of programmed cell death characterized by inflammation and cell lysis, typically triggered by the activation of inflammasomes. Copper overload can activate inflammasomes, leading to the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-1β and IL-18) and triggering cell rupture. This process is often associated with caspase-1 activation and the subsequent formation of pores in the cell membrane, which leads to cell swelling and lysis.30 Pyroptosis is typically triggered by pathogen infection but can also occur in response to cellular stress, such as copper-induced damage. In macrophages and other immune cells, copper-induced oxidative stress can activate the NLR family pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome, a critical sensor for cellular stress.31 Upon activation, the inflammasome activates caspase-1, leading to the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and pyroptotic cell death (Figure 5).

Autophagy is a cellular process by which cells degrade and recycle damaged or unnecessary organelles and proteins to maintain homeostasis. However, under oxidative stress, excess copper can impair autophagic flux, preventing the clearance of damaged organelles, such as mitochondria, and accumulating toxic cellular components.7,32 Excessive copper can overload the lysosomal system and impair the degradation of toxic substances.33 This dysregulation can lead to the accumulation of damaged organelles (such as mitochondria) and proteins, which may induce autophagic cell death (type II programmed cell death). In such cases, cells may die due to failure to clear copper-induced damage effectively (Figure 5).

In summary, excessive copper accumulation can trigger various forms of cell death, each of which contributes to cellular damage in distinct ways. These mechanisms reveal the multifaceted nature of copper-induced toxicity, which can impair essential cellular functions such as mitochondrial integrity and the immune response. Understanding the intricate mechanisms through which copper exerts its toxic effects is critical for developing targeted therapeutic approaches. Future research should focus on elucidating the molecular interactions among these cell death pathways, as such insights could lead to the development of novel treatments for diseases associated with copper dysregulation. Moreover, therapeutic strategies aimed at selectively modulating specific cell death pathways may offer more precise and effective interventions for managing copper-induced toxicity and its related pathological conditions.

sec.title: Mechanism of cuproptosis
sec.p: The term “cuproptosis” was first introduced by Tsvetkov et al. in their groundbreaking 2022 research, which unveiled a novel form of regulated cell death distinct from other known mechanisms.2 A significant feature of Cu-treated cells is a sharp increase in ROS levels, which has long been considered the main cause of cell death. ROS scavengers, such as N-acetylcysteine (NAC), can mitigate the extent of Cu-induced cell death in some cells.34 However, eliminating ROS does not always inhibit cell death induced by Cu, suggesting that ROS are not primary mediators of Cu-induced cell death. Tsvetkov et al. further demonstrated that antioxidants were ineffective in rescuing cells from damage caused by elesclomol (a copper ionophore) combined with copper.2 Furthermore, treatment with inhibitors of other known cell death mechanisms, including ferroptosis (ferrostatin-1), necroptosis (necrostatin-1), and oxidative stress (N-acetyl cysteine) all failed to abrogate copper ionophore-induced cell death, suggesting a mechanism distinct from known cell death pathways.

The copper ionophore, such as elesclomol, can transport Cu2+ into mitochondria, where Cu2+ is reduced to Cu+ by ferredoxin 1 (FDX1). Accumulated Cu+ in mitochondria induces dihydrolipoamide S-acetyltransferase (DLAT) aggregation by directly binding to lipoylated DLAT, ultimately triggering mitochondrial proteotoxic stress and resulting in cuproptosis. At the same time, excess copper ions can replace or disrupt the iron in Fe–S clusters, destabilizing Fe–S proteins. This degradation of Fe–S proteins interrupts the electron transport chain, leading to mitochondrial damage, energy depletion, and ultimately cell death via cuproptosis (Figure 6). FDX1 is a small, iron‒sulfur cluster-containing protein located primarily in the mitochondria, where it plays a crucial role in various metabolic processes, particularly in cuproptosis. It carries out two functions in cuproptosis. First, FDX1 is a mitochondrial matrix reductase that reduces ionophore-brought copper ions from Cu2+ to Cu+.35 Second, FDX1 promotes protein lipoylation by directly binding to lipoic acid synthetase (LIAS) to facilitate its maturation and stabilize its enzyme activity.36 FDX1, as an electron carrier, provides the necessary electrons for LIAS to catalyze the conversion of intermediates into lipoic acid. These electrons facilitate the reduction reactions required by LIAS to generate the lipoyl moiety. The lipoyl moiety is the sulfur-containing group required for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHC) activity.37 Once synthesized, lipoic acid is transferred by lipoic acid transferase 1 (LIPT1) to specific lysine residues on DLAT, a core component of the PDH complex's E2 subunit.38 This sequence of interactions enables the PDHC to convert pyruvate into acetyl-CoA, which is a fundamental substrate for the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle39 (Figure 6). FDX1, serving as an upstream regulator in the lipoic acid pathway, plays a crucial role in the TCA cycle and ensures efficient cellular energy production. This highlights its unique importance in maintaining mitochondrial function, metabolic homeostasis, and overall cellular health.

caption.p: Mechanism of cuproptosis. Extracellular copper, often delivered via copper ionophores such as elesclomol, enters cells and is reduced to Cu+ within mitochondria by FDX1. Accumulated Cu+ in mitochondria induces DLAT aggregation by directly binding to lipoylated DLAT and destabilizes Fe–S cluster proteins, ultimately triggering mitochondrial proteotoxic stress and resulting in cuproptosis.

sec.p: Fe‒S (iron‒sulfur) clusters are fragile assemblies within proteins that play critical roles in mitochondrial function, especially in the electron transport chain (ETC). These clusters often contain iron atoms coordinated with sulfur, forming a stable structure essential for electron transfer. Fe–S clusters are essential for the stability and function of the Fe–S proteins involved in the ETC. The degradation of Fe–S clusters caused by copper results in the dysfunction of these proteins. This degradation reduces the electron-carrying capacity of the ETC complexes, interrupting the flow of electrons through the chain. The ETC fails to operate properly, and ATP synthesis through oxidative phosphorylation is reduced. This dysfunction leads to mitochondrial stress, causing damage to mitochondrial membranes, further impairing cellular energy metabolism and ultimately leading to cell death. Cuproptosis is distinct from apoptosis and necrosis, relying instead on mitochondrial toxicity and metabolic collapse triggered by copper's interaction with Fe–S clusters and proteins.

sec.title: Copper and cuproptosis in digestive system cancers
Esophageal cancer
sec.p: Esophageal carcinoma (ESCA) is a serious malignancy. In 2022, the incidence ranked 11th, and the mortality ranked 7th among all malignancies.1 Owing to the lack of early diagnosis, most patients (around 75%) are diagnosed at late stages, which resulted in only about 20%–25% five-year survival rate for decades without great improvement.40 At the same time, only a few therapeutic targets, including epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), are available for treating ESCA. Therefore, exploring the mechanisms, early markers, and therapeutic targets of esophageal cancer is crucial.

It is well known that heat stress is considered one of the risk factors in the etiology of ESCA. Cells would suffer a series of primary and secondary injury or even death with metabolic abnormalities once overheated. Jiang et al. identified key cell death genes, including cuproptosis-related pyruvate dehydrogenase complex component X (PDHX) and heat stress-related heat shock protein family D (Hsp60) member 1 (HSPD1), involved in ESCA progression using TCGA-ESCA database. They found that cuproptosis might be a risk factor for heat stress-related ESCA, with HSPD1 and PDHX overexpression predicting poor outcomes. These genes affect tumor activity via cellular stemness, metabolism, and immune suppression. This study highlights the significance of the “heat stress‒cuproptosis” pattern in ESCA, suggesting new therapeutic avenues.41 However, larger prospective studies and further functional experiments are needed to validate these findings and elucidate the mechanisms. The process of cuproptosis, a copper-dependent form of cell death, occurs when copper directly binds to lipoylated proteins within the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, particularly affecting enzymes such as the PDHC. In ESCA cells, this disruption of PDHC activity by copper binding severely impairs cellular metabolism. Rather than merely halting energy production, this disruption initiates a cascade of toxic protein aggregation, ultimately leading to cell death. This cascade of events underscores the critical role of cuproptosis in ESCA progression and highlights potential therapeutic targets for intervention. Wu and colleagues employed bioinformatics analysis to construct a risk scoring model based on cuproptosis genes (CUGs), which can effectively predict the survival and prognosis of patients with esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC). They discovered that a drug named Milciclib may inhibit the proliferation and migration of certain ESCC cells by targeting cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor 2A (CDKN2A), offering a new direction for the potential treatment of ESCC.42

sec.title: Gastric cancer
sec.p: Gastric cancer ranks 5th in both incidence and mortality among all types of cancer. Gastric cancer poses a significant global health burden, with nearly one million new cases reported in 2022, resulting in approximately 660,000 deaths.1 Despite significant improvements in surgical procedures and chemotherapy treatments, the outlook for individuals with advanced gastric cancer continues to be grim. In the past decade, while there has been a notable increase in the five-year overall age-standardized relative survival rate for gastric cancer, it remains below 50%.43 Immunotherapy, primarily employed as a salvage treatment for patients ineligible for surgery, has demonstrated efficacy in improving the three-year survival rate. However, the overall survival rate remains relatively low. The goal of non-surgical tumor treatment is to induce tumor cell apoptosis or necrosis and to activate the body's immune system to eradicate cancer cells. Unfortunately, tumor cells exhibit significant resistance to these processes, which is believed to be associated with the accumulation of metal ions in the tumor microenvironment. A growing body of research has highlighted the role of metal ions, particularly copper, in the development and progression of gastric cancers. Copper, an essential trace element, plays a crucial role in a wide range of cellular processes, including mitochondrial function, antioxidant defense, immune infiltration and angiogenesis.

The mitochondria are central to the pathophysiology of cuproptosis. Copper ions, when in excess, disrupt mitochondrial function by binding to lipoylated enzymes in the TCA cycle.44 In gastric cancer, alterations in mitochondrial metabolism are common, and many cancer cells exhibit a reliance on altered TCA cycle activity or switch to aerobic glycolysis (the Warburg effect) to meet their energy demands.45 This reliance on the mitochondrial and glycolytic pathways makes gastric cancer cells particularly sensitive to copper-induced damage. Excess copper disrupts the function of these enzymes, leading to metabolic collapse and the activation of cuproptosis. The Chen group reported a study on gastric cancer metabolic subtypes, in which they classified gastric cancers into three distinct subtypes based on metabolic gene signatures and systematically characterized the clinical and metabolic features of these subtypes. They constructed a metabolic subtype-related prognosis genes (MSPGs) model to predict prognosis and the immune response. They found that in gastric cancer subtypes with high metabolic scores, the activity of the cuproptosis pathway is increased, along with elevated expression levels of cuproptosis-related proteins. These findings also suggested that these subtypes might have been more sensitive to copper chelator therapy. This discovery not only provides a new perspective for understanding the metabolic heterogeneity of gastric cancer but also offers potential targets for the development of novel therapeutic strategies targeting specific metabolic subtypes.46

In gastric cancer, the interaction between copper metabolism and immune responses is particularly significant, as the tumor microenvironment in gastric cancer is often characterized by chronic inflammation, immune evasion, and altered immune cell infiltration. Recent studies suggest that cuproptosis, by inducing specific metabolic disruptions within tumor cells, may have a profound impact on immune cell activity and the overall immune landscape of gastric cancer. Tang et al. conducted a comprehensive analysis of the cuproptosis-related human copper proteome in gastric cancer patients, establishing a Cu-binding proteins (CBP) signature associated with patient survival and the immune microenvironment. The study found that the CBP signature is an independent prognostic indicator, with patients having lower CBP scores showing more anti-tumor immune cells and a better response to immunotherapy. Researchers have also found that patients with CBP-low have higher levels of TMB and MSI-H, and the clinical outcomes indicate that these patients respond better to immunotherapy using PD-1 blockade. Unfortunately, patients with CBP-low are not sensitive to the response to chemotherapy.47 Therefore, the CBP signature is associated with tumor immune infiltration and can be used for guiding individual treatment for patients with GC. Dong et al. conducted a study on cuproptosis-related genes (CRGs) in gastric cancer and developed a prognostic signature (CRG-score) to predict patient survival. Similarly, they found significant differences in immune cell infiltration within the tumor microenvironment (TME), immune checkpoint expression, HLA expression, and sensitivity to immunotherapy between different CRG-score groups. A high CRG-score was featured with decreased microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) and mutational burden. Meanwhile, the CRG-score was significantly related to the cancer stem cell (CSC) index and chemotherapeutic response.48 Another study found a significant correlation between various immune cells and copper death-related genes (CRGs) in the TME. Specifically, GLS is negatively correlated with regulatory T cells (Tregs), resting NK cells, and neutrophils; CDKN2A is negatively correlated with naive B cells and plasma cells; LIAS is positively correlated with CD4 memory-activated T cells, and negatively correlated with M0- and M1-type macrophages.49 Cuproptosis is not only a novel mechanism of cell death in gastric cancer but also plays a significant role in shaping the immune microenvironment. By influencing immune cell infiltration, polarization, and activation, cuproptosis can potentially modulate the anti-tumor immune response, either by promoting immune activation or by counteracting immune suppression. Further research into the specific mechanisms linking cuproptosis to immune cell dynamics in gastric cancer could offer new insights into immune-based therapies and highlight copper as a promising target for cancer treatment.

Angiogenesis is a physiological process that is co-regulated by pro-angiogenic factors and anti-angiogenic factors. It is characterized by the formation of new blood vessels from pre-existing vasculature, which is a key process for tumor growth and metastasis, providing essential nutrients and oxygen for rapidly growing tumors. The concept of Cu2+ pro-angiogenesis was first proposed by Professor McAuslan and Reilly.50 In experiments on copper-induced phagocytosis of aortic endothelial cells, they found that mutant aortic endothelial cell lines were highly sensitive to copper. Under experimental conditions, endothelial cells are migrated by copper salts, traveling up to 24 mm in 1000 h. Based on this phenomenon, this study proposed copper ions as a model system for early neovascular events and as a sensitive assay for detecting angiogenic activity. Copper is an essential cofactor for the activation of metalloproteases. Under physiological pH conditions in humans, copper binds to angiopoietin molecules, thereby enhancing their binding to endothelial cells. When the balance tips in favor of proangiogenic molecules over anti-angiogenic ones, angiogenesis is triggered, leading to the early stages of new blood vessel formation. This process, if hijacked by cancer cells, can contribute to tumor angiogenesis, thereby facilitating the metastasis and progression of cancer.51 However, cuproptosis induces profound metabolic disturbances within cells, particularly affecting mitochondrial function. In gastric cancer, this disruption can significantly impact the production and secretion of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), a key mediator of angiogenesis. VEGF expression in tumors is primarily driven by hypoxic conditions and metabolic stress, which are common in rapidly growing tumors that exceed their existing blood supply. Recent studies suggest that the induction of cuproptosis in cancer cells disrupts their metabolic state, altering the hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) pathway, which is a major regulator of VEGF. Copper accumulation in cells undergoing cuproptosis could inhibit HIF-1α stabilization and activity, thereby reducing VEGF expression. This reduction in VEGF levels can lead to a decrease in angiogenic signaling and a reduction in the formation of new blood vessels, creating a less favorable environment for tumor growth and metastasis.52 He et al. found through single-cell sequencing analysis that most CRGs are primarily upregulated in endothelial cells and are related to pathways associated with angiogenesis.53 Cuproptosis holds significant potential in inhibiting tumor angiogenesis, slowing tumor growth, and preventing metastasis, thus warranting further in-depth investigation by researchers.

Chronic infection with the bacterium Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) stands as the principal risk factor for gastric cancer, accounting for an estimated 90% of cases.54 Chronic infection with H. pylori leads to inflammation, epithelial cell damage, and eventually, carcinogenesis. Recent studies have suggested that cuproptosis can significantly affect the levels of Cu ions, which can then impact the interaction between H. pylori and the gastric epithelium, influencing the course of infection and contributing to gastric cancer. However, studies on copper ions and H. pylori are also full of contradictions. The Montefusco group reported that copper ions promote the formation of trefoil factor 1 (TFF1) homodimers, which not only enhances TFF1's ability to facilitate H. pylori colonization but also significantly increase the number of H. pylori colonizing AGS-AC1 cells that highly express TFF1. Furthermore, copper treatment increases the thickness of the mucin layer produced by HT29-E12 cells, potentially providing more binding sites for H. pylori and thereby further enhancing its colonization of gastric epithelial cells.55 The findings of Esposito's study contradict those of Montefusco, with their conclusions being inconsistent with each other. Esposito and colleagues found that H. pylori infection significantly reduced the concentration of copper in the stomachs of mice without affecting the levels in their bloodstream. In copper-deficient conditions, the TFF1 gene was induced, which may have played a role in resisting H. pylori infection by making it difficult for bacteria to colonize epithelial cells, thereby reducing gastric damage in mice. Furthermore, the study observed that the condition of the stomach in mice deteriorated after the use of copper chelators such as tetrathiomolybdate (TM) due to the silencing of the TFF1 gene by H. pylori, which could not be restored.56 Nevertheless, the results in human tissues show different patterns. Aslan et al. used inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP–OES) to determine the levels of 25 trace elements in serum and tissue samples. They found that there was no significant difference in copper ion levels in the serum and endoscopic biopsy tissues between patients infected with H. pylori and those not infected.57 Based on the mechanism of interaction between copper and H. pylori, it may be possible to further utilize cuproptosis to trigger the cytotoxicity and stress-induced death of H. pylori, thereby discovering a new approach to reduce the risk of gastric cancer development. The emerging understanding of cuproptosis as a copper-induced form of cell death presents a novel opportunity for targeting gastric cancer. As the understanding of copper homeostasis and cuproptosis pathways continues to evolve, the development of copper-targeted therapies holds significant promise for improving the treatment of gastric cancer.

sec.title: Colorectal cancer
sec.p: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is one of the most common malignancies worldwide, with a rising incidence rate due to lifestyle factors and an aging population. In 2022, colorectal cancer ranked third in incidence among all types of cancer, while its mortality ranked second, behind only lung cancer.1 Copper is an essential micronutrient that is absorbed through the gastrointestinal tract and is involved in several enzymatic reactions crucial for normal cellular functions. The Baszuk team conducted a retrospective analysis comparing the serum copper levels of 187 colorectal cancer patients with those of a healthy control group. The study found that the blood copper levels in the colorectal cancer patient group were significantly higher than those in the control group, with the most notable differences observed in the top quartile of copper levels. Furthermore, higher blood copper levels were associated with advanced-stage (III–IV) and high-grade (G3) tumors compared to early stage (I–II) and low-grade (G1–G2) tumors.58

In colorectal cancer, the metabolic reprogramming of cells frequently alters copper homeostasis. On the one hand, tumor cells tend to have higher copper uptake, possibly due to the increased activity of copper transporters, which supports the heightened metabolic and bioenergetic demands of rapidly proliferating cancer cells. On the other hand, copper levels in the tumor microenvironment can influence angiogenesis and immune responses, further contributing to cancer progression. Antioxidant 1 copper chaperone (ATOX1) is a key protein involved in copper homeostasis, acting as a copper chaperone that binds to copper ions and facilitates their safe transport within cells.59 In cancer, however, disruptions in copper metabolism can lead to the overexpression of ATOX1, which significantly impacts cancer cell proliferation, particularly in colorectal cancer. Meanwhile, ATOX1 also serves as a transcription factor that regulates the expression of Cyclin D1 and promotes cell proliferation by binding to copper.60 In addition, ATOX1 can inhibit ROS production and increase ATP production by decreasing the expression of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) oxidase subunit p47phox, thereby creating conditions for colorectal cancer development and cell proliferation.61 Tumor cells require higher levels of copper to support increased cellular proliferation, angiogenesis, and oxidative stress management.

The tumor microenvironment is a complex ecosystem in which tumor cells interact with immune cells, the extracellular matrix, and various signaling molecules. Epithelial‒mesenchymal transition (EMT) is a key event in cancer metastasis, endowing tumor cells with mesenchymal characteristics and enhancing their invasive and metastatic capabilities. During this process, tumor cells may alter their immune phenotypes, thereby affecting the patterns of immune cell infiltration. Immune infiltration refers to the distribution and accumulation of immune cells within tumor tissues, which not only reflects the tumor's ability to attract the immune system but also indicates potential immune evasion strategies of the tumor. Copper's role in EMT is closely linked to the dynamics of the TME. Elevated copper levels in the TME can enhance the activity of matrix remodeling enzymes, such as lysyl oxidase (LOX), and increase oxidative stress, which alters the cellular interactions between cancer cells and stromal cells.62 LOX plays an essential role in the crosslinking of collagen and elastin fibers in the extracellular matrix (ECM). In colorectal cancer, LOX facilitates the detachment of cancer cells from the primary tumor site and their invasion into adjacent tissues by inducing the reorganization of the ECM, thereby establishing secondary tumor foci at a distance. The TGF-β signaling pathway also plays a central role in EMT. Copper-mediated ROS production and LOX activity contribute to TGF-β signaling activation, which leads to the phosphorylation and activation of SMAD proteins that drive mesenchymal gene expression.63 TGF-β is also implicated in the regulation of immune cells within the TME, further promoting an environment that supports EMT and tumor progression.64

Cuproptosis has also been considered to have a close association with tumor metabolism, especially the TCA cycle. Metabolic reprogramming occurs throughout the process of malignant transformation to meet the demands of the TME and unique tumor cell growth. Wang et al. investigated the function of the cuproptosis-related gene FDX1 in colorectal cancer, focusing on its ability to suppress tumor growth and advancement by slowing down the EMT process.65 In recent years, there has been a significant surge in bioinformatics research focusing on cuproptosis in colorectal cancer, reflecting a growing scientific interest in unraveling the role and impact of this copper-dependent cell death mechanism in the field of oncology, particularly within the context of the tumor microenvironment. Numerous literature indicates that most cuproptosis-related genes are differentially expressed between CRC tissues and normal tissues. The research results of Wu and colleagues are consistent with this.66 Meanwhile, their research also reveals the correlation between CRGs and the levels of immune cells and stromal cells in the tumor microenvironment, offering a new perspective for the prognostic assessment of CRC. Additionally, the risk scoring system they developed based on CRGs exhibits a good predictive value for the prognosis of patients with CRC.66 The Shao team also developed a new scoring system, the CuproScore, which is based on cuproptosis-related markers. This system can effectively distinguish and predict the prognosis, TME characteristics, and response to immunotherapy in colorectal cancer patients. These findings reveal the potential of cuproptosis as a therapeutic target in colorectal cancer, especially in the context of immunotherapy and possible combination treatment strategies.67 Similarly, Zeng et al. established a cuproptosis model for microsatellite stable (MSS) colon cancer. They performed immune reconstitution in NOD SCID gamma (NSG) mice to assess the impact of cuproptosis on immune infiltration and cancer progression in MSS colon cancer mouse models. The results showed that cuproptosis increased the infiltration of CD8+ T cells into tumor tissues and delayed cancer progression, which may be achieved by reducing the expression of PD-L1.68 Inducing cuproptosis in the CRC TME presents a novel therapeutic strategy that exploits the unique vulnerabilities of cancer cells to copper dysregulation. Understanding how cuproptosis can be initiated and regulated in the TME could lead to the development of targeted therapies that effectively reduce tumor viability while potentially enhancing anti-tumor immunity. Future research should focus on refining our understanding of copper metabolism in CRC, optimizing cuproptosis induction methods, and elucidating the broader impacts of this form of cell death on the tumor microenvironment.

sec.title: Pancreatic cancer
sec.p: The pancreas aids in digestion and regulates blood sugar levels through its exocrine and endocrine functions. The majority of pancreatic cancers originate in exocrine cells, which are responsible for the production of digestive enzymes. Among these, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is the most prevalent, accounting for more than 90% of all pancreatic carcinomas. The prognosis for pancreatic cancer remains poor, with a 5-y survival rate of no more than 10% due to late diagnosis and the aggressive nature of the disease.69 Traditional treatment modalities such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy often fail to yield significant long-term survival benefits because of the aggressive nature of the disease and its resistance mechanisms.

Yao et al. found that the serum copper concentration in patients with pancreatic cancer is significantly higher than that in healthy individuals, indicating that high concentrations of copper may be associated with an increased risk of pancreatic cancer.70 Copper is a critical cofactor for several enzymes that participate in oxidative stress regulation, energy metabolism, and cell proliferation, all of which are crucial in cancer development and progression. In pancreatic cancer, the disruption of copper metabolism leads to an accumulation of copper ions in the tumor microenvironment, which in turn increases the sensitivity of tumor cells to copper-induced oxidative stress and toxicity. The increased copper levels in pancreatic cancer are primarily due to the overexpression of copper transporters such as CTR1, ATP7A, and ATP7B.71 These transporters facilitate copper uptake and efflux in cells, ensuring that the correct levels of copper are available for cellular processes.72 SLC31A1 is a gene that encodes the protein CTR1. The Zhang team conducted an in-depth analysis of the role of the SLC31A1 gene across nine types of cancer using a suite of bioinformatics tools. This study revealed significant expression differences of SLC31A1 between various tumor tissues and normal tissues, with the highest mutation rate of the SLC31A1 gene observed in pancreatic cancer. This increased availability of copper is associated with enhanced activity of copper-dependent enzymes, which are involved in pathways that promote cancer cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and metastatic potential.73

The hallmark of cuproptosis is the direct binding of excess copper to mitochondrial proteins, which leads to mitochondrial dysfunction and cell death. This interaction causes the destabilization of these enzymes, resulting in mitochondrial dysfunction and the production of ROS, which are key drivers of cellular damage. Huang et al. reported that they conducted a comprehensive study on the therapeutic effects of eupalinolide B (EB) against pancreatic cancer. The study demonstrated that EB effectively inhibits the viability, proliferation, migration, and invasion of pancreatic cancer cells through the generation of ROS and the induction of potential cuproptosis.74 Cuproptosis not only leads to cell death but also affects the progression of pancreatic cancer by regulating the expression of long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs).75 LncRNAs are a class of RNA molecules longer than 200 nucleotides that do not code for proteins but are involved in various cellular processes, including gene regulation, metabolic regulation, and signal transduction. LncRNAs play crucial roles in the regulation of various processes that contribute to pancreatic cancer progression, including cell proliferation, metastasis, and chemoresistance. The Yao team constructed a risk signature based on the expression of five lncRNAs related to cuproptosis, which can accurately predict the prognosis of pancreatic cancer patients and reveal the key role of cancer susceptibility 8 (CASC8) in tumor progression and immune microenvironment characterization.76 Meanwhile, Chen and colleagues investigated 672 lncRNAs related to cuproptosis in pancreatic cancer, highlighting that increased expression of LINC00853 is associated with enhanced glycolysis and proliferation in pancreatic cancer cells, with this effect being mediated by 6-phosphofructo-2-kinase/fructose-2,6-biphosphatase 3 (PFKFB3). These studies reveal the critical regulatory roles of lncRNAs in pancreatic cancer, laying the foundation for the further development of lncRNA-based precision therapeutic strategies.77 The Huang group developed a cuproptosis-related lncRNA scoring system to predict the clinical outcomes and immune landscape in pancreatic cancer, and provide a reference for precise therapeutic drug selection.78 Similarly, Sun et al. have developed a novel prognostic model based on cuproptosis-related lncRNAs derived from single-cell data, which is capable of predicting pancreatic cancer patient prognosis and the immune landscape. They have also validated the efficacy of a combined treatment approach consisting of immunotherapy, targeted therapy, and chemotherapy in animal models, providing a new direction for personalized treatment.79 However, further studies are required to fully understand the precise molecular mechanisms of cuproptosis in pancreatic cancer and to develop safe and effective therapeutic strategies. This emerging field holds great promise for improving treatment outcomes in a cancer that is notoriously resistant to current therapies.

sec.title: Liver cancer
sec.p: Liver cancer is a prevalent malignant tumor, ranking as the third leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide.1 It is primarily categorized into primary and secondary types, with primary liver cancer being more common, accounting for 75%–85% of cases. Among primary liver cancers, hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the most frequent, constituting 85%–90% of cases.80 The major risk factors for HCC include chronic infections with hepatitis B virus (HBV) and hepatitis C virus (HCV), alcohol abuse, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD).81 The five-year survival rate for HCC is typically below 15%, making the search for new diagnostic and therapeutic approaches are crucial in HCC research.

The liver plays a central role in copper metabolism by synthesizing and secreting copper-binding proteins and regulating copper homeostasis. Owing to its central role in copper metabolism, the liver is particularly vulnerable to copper imbalance. Excess copper can cause liver damage and Wilson's disease, while copper deficiency is often observed in lipid metabolic disorders, including non-alcoholic liver disease and alcoholic cirrhosis.82 In normal conditions, copper is incorporated into ceruloplasmin, a copper-binding protein, in the liver, and exported to the bloodstream. Additionally, hepatic copper is also stored in metallothioneins (MTs) to prevent toxic accumulation. The regulation of copper metabolism is orchestrated through a feedback mechanism involving the expression of genes encoding copper transporters, chaperones, and storage proteins. This regulatory process ensures a balance between copper intake, intracellular storage, and efflux. When copper metabolism is disrupted, hepatocytes are the first cells to be affected. Studies have shown that Wilson's disease, characterized by defective copper processing, results in elevated copper levels, which are toxic to liver cells.83 Additionally, the incidence of HCC is significantly higher in patients with Wilson's disease, further suggesting that copper overload may promote malignant transformation.84 Furthermore, serum copper levels in HCC patients are markedly higher than those in patients with chronic hepatitis, and the copper concentration correlates with the incidence and progression of HCC.85 These clinical observations indicate that copper may play a carcinogenic role in the pathogenesis of HCC. Current research suggests that copper promotes liver tumorigenesis by affecting angiogenesis, increasing ROS production, and disrupting metabolic homeostasis.

In cancer, angiogenesis supports tumor growth and metastasis by providing oxygen and nutrients to rapidly proliferating tumor cells. Copper is known as a “switch-on” angiogenic messenger that promotes the proliferation and mobility of vascular endothelial cells, which are critical initial steps in the course of tumor angiogenesis.86 Endothelial cells line blood vessels and are crucial for the formation of new capillaries. Copper regulates endothelial cell function by interacting with specific copper-dependent enzymes and signaling molecules. LOX activity promotes endothelial cell migration and proliferation by altering the ECM structure, which provides a suitable substrate for angiogenesis.87 Wong et al. reported that lysyl oxidase like 2 (LOXL2) plays a critical role in the tumor microenvironment and metastatic niche formation in HCC. This study elucidated a complex signaling network regulating LOXL2 expression involving hypoxia, TGF-β, and specific miRNAs and demonstrated the function of LOXL2 in modifying the extracellular matrix, promoting HCC cell cytoskeletal reorganization, and enhancing endothelial cell migration.88 Furthermore, intracellular copper has been demonstrated to stabilize the structure of HIF-1, thereby enhancing its transcriptional activity on genes involved in angiogenesis, including the VEGF and ceruloplasmin genes. Ceruloplasmin, a serum globulin, stimulates new blood vessel formation by binding with copper ions, whereas its copper-deficient form is unable to induce angiogenesis.89 As for VEGF, experiments have shown that in cultured human cardiomyocytes, copper ions at a concentration of 5 μM can enhance VEGF expression induced by insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1).90 Interestingly, Das et al. found that CTR1 promotes VEGF-induced VEGFR2 signaling and angiogenesis in endothelial cells through a mechanism unrelated to copper transport. This study ultimately demonstrated that CTR1 acts as a redox sensor, playing a novel, atypical role in ROS sensing and highlighting its important function in angiogenesis.86 While this finding suggests that CTR1-induced angiogenesis is not related to copper transport, the increase in intracellular copper ions could still lead to the generation of ROS. Higher copper levels in the liver can lead to ROS production, alter metabolic pathways, and promote HCC progression. Elevated copper levels enhance mitochondrial ATP production and oxidative phosphorylation, thus contributing to increased energy availability for cancer cell proliferation. Dysregulated copper metabolism in HCC has been linked to enhanced mitochondrial activity, which not only supports ATP production but also generates excess ROS. The mitochondria in cancer cells undergo significant damage due to ROS, including mutations in mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA). These mutations further impair mitochondrial function and enhance the production of ROS, leading to a vicious cycle that drives tumorigenesis.91 Understanding the mechanisms through which copper-induced ROS contribute to liver cancer may provide new insights into potential therapeutic strategies targeting copper homeostasis and oxidative stress.

While elevated copper levels can promote tumor growth and angiogenesis, excessive copper accumulation may trigger the aggregation of mitochondrial enzymes and the loss of Fe–S proteins, leading to a unique form of cell death known as cuproptosis. This process has therapeutic potential. Key enzymes and genes involved in cuproptosis have become important targets for liver cancer therapy, and the exploration of cuproptosis in the diagnosis, treatment, prognosis, and survival analysis of HCC has become a major research focus. The Ding group reported a study on cuproptosis-related subtypes and the cuproptosis-related gene prognostic index (CRGPI) in HCC. By analyzing the expression and mutation status of ten CRGs, the study identified three cuproptosis subtypes in HCC and constructed the CRGPI to predict the prognosis and response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in HCC patients.92 Similarly, Zhang and colleagues explored not only the correlation between the expression levels of FDX1 and the prognosis of HCC patients but also developed an innovative risk scoring system, the cuproptosis-related risk score (CRRS). CRRS showed high accuracy in predicting the efficacy of sorafenib and the non-responsiveness to transcatheter arterial chemoembolization (TACE) in HCC patients.93 Cuproptosis impacts the functionality of mitochondria by disrupting the TCA cycle within them, thereby regulating cellular metabolic activities and significantly influencing tumor development. Li et al. have uncovered that maternal embryonic leucine zipper kinase (MELK) promotes the progression of HCC by enhancing the expression of the cuproptosis-related gene DLAT through the activation of the PI3K/mTOR signaling pathway, thereby impacting mitochondrial function. This discovery offers a potential new therapeutic target for HCC treatment.27 Xing and colleagues utilized CRISPR‒Cas9 synthetic lethal screening to uncover that ArId1A-deficient HCC cells are highly dependent on the TCA cycle and that these cells shift from aerobic glycolysis to reliance on the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation by downregulating pyruvate kinase M (PKM) expression. This study innovatively proposes targeting the TCA cycle through cuproptosis as a potential therapeutic strategy for ArId1A-deficient HCC, which has been validated through in vitro and in vivo experiments. However, research is still lacking in safety assessment of copper therapy, and further studies are needed to explore its potential clinical applications.94 Establishing predictive models for genes associated with cuproptosis can effectively forecast the prognosis of HCC patients. Moreover, targeting cuproptosis to regulate mitochondrial metabolism offers a new approach for cancer treatment. These studies open up new horizons for the study of metabolic molecular mechanisms and targeted therapy in liver cancer. Nonetheless, only a few hypotheses have been validated by biological techniques, and research on cuproptosis still faces some limitations.

sec.title: Biliary tract cancer
sec.p: Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA) is a highly heterogeneous malignant tumor of the liver and bile ducts, which typically arises from the malignant transformation of cholangiocytes. It is a rare and fatal disease, accounting for nearly 3% of gastrointestinal cancers and 15% of primary liver cancers.95 In recent years, both the incidence and mortality rates of CCA have been rising. Due to the lack of typical early signs and specific screening or diagnostic markers, most patients are diagnosed at advanced stages of the disease. While surgical resection remains the only curative option, only about 30% of patients are eligible for surgery.96 Furthermore, the high postoperative recurrence rate (50%–60%) results in a 5-y survival rate of less than 45%. Therefore, effective pharmacological treatments are critical for patients who are not candidates for surgery or those who experience postoperative recurrence. Recent studies have shown that cuproptosis plays an important role in the occurrence, development and anti-tumor process. This provides a new direction for the early diagnosis and targeted therapy of cholangiocarcinoma. The Chen team established a prognosis model for cholangiocarcinoma based on CRGs, which includes four key genes: ATP7A, FDX1, dihydrolipoamide branched chain transacylase E2 (DBT), and LIAS. This model is capable of independently predicting the prognosis of CCA patients. Moreover, the study revealed the significant inhibitory effect of cuproptosis on the activity of CCA cells, particularly the alleviation of cuproptosis-induced toxicity in CCA cells through the knockdown of the LIAS gene.97 Similarly, He et al. also developed a scoring system based on CRGs that can effectively predict the prognosis of patients with cholangiocarcinoma. The study particularly highlighted that the glycine cleavage system protein H (GCSH) gene plays a pivotal role in regulating cuproptosis in cholangiocarcinoma and may modulate the malignancy of the tumor by affecting the JAK-STAT signaling pathway.98 Other study has shown that cholangiocarcinoma patients can be classified into low-risk and high-risk groups based on a risk scoring system derived from CRGs. These findings indicate that patients in the low-risk group have significantly higher survival rates compared to those in the high-risk group. Notably, there are significant differences between the two groups in terms of the tumor microenvironment, the proportion of endothelial cells, the levels of cancer-associated fibroblasts, and their sensitivity to chemotherapy drugs and immune checkpoint inhibitors.99

Gallbladder cancer (GBC) is a malignant tumor originating from the mucosal epithelial cells of the gallbladder, which is a common malignant tumor in the biliary system. Although research on the relationship between gallbladder cancer and cuproptosis is relatively scarce, studies have indicated that copper plays an important role in the occurrence and development of GBC. The Basu team investigated the levels of copper in the serum, bile, gallbladder stones, and gallbladder tissue of patients with gallbladder cancer and cholelithiasis, discovering that copper levels were elevated in patients with gallbladder cancer, a phenomenon also observed in bile and gallbladder tissue.100 Lee et al. conducted a larger-scale study and found a significant positive correlation between serum copper levels and gallbladder cancer as well as gallstones.101 These all suggest that increased copper levels may be associated with the occurrence of gallbladder cancer, providing significant clues for further exploration of the role of copper in gallbladder cancer.

sec.title: Cuproptosis-based targeted therapy for digestive system cancers
sec.p: In digestive system cancers, elevated copper levels have been implicated in tumor progression, metastasis, and resistance to conventional therapies. The discovery of cuproptosis presents a novel therapeutic avenue, particularly in cancers where copper metabolism is aberrant. In digestive system cancers, alterations in copper homeostasis are a significant characteristic, indicating that understanding the relationship between cuproptosis and copper metabolic disorders is crucial for developing targeted treatment strategies. This could bring hope for more effective treatments for patients with these aggressive malignant tumors.

sec.title: Copper chelators
sec.p: Copper chelators are compounds that bind to copper ions, preventing them from interacting with cellular copper-dependent enzymes and proteins. By reducing intracellular copper availability, these agents induce cuproptosis by disrupting mitochondrial functions, such as electron transport and oxidative phosphorylation. Some representative copper chelators with anti-cancer activity include tetrathiomolybdate (TTM), trientine, and penicillamine, which are known not only for their chemical structures but also for their applications in clinical trials (Tables 1 and 3).

caption.p: Different copper chelators for digestive system cancer treatment.

tr.td: Tetrathiomolybdate with lenvatinib significantly reduces angiogenesis in liver tumors
A combination treatment with a copper chelator TM and an autophagy inhibitor CQ was found to increase cell death in vitro and retard cancer growth in vivo
Trientine can potently suppress tumor growth and angiogenesis
Trientine inhibits the production of interleukin-8 (IL-8), thereby suppressing tumor growth, enhancing the apoptotic potential of tumor cells, and effectively reducing tumor microvascular density
The silencing of the ATOX1 gene may enhance the anticancer therapeutic effect of the copper chelator TPEN
TPEN selectively kills cancer cells by chelating copper to produce reactive oxygen species without harming normal cells

JYFY-001 not only demonstrates a potent inhibitory effect on the proliferation of colorectal cancer cells, inducing apoptosis, but also enhances the antitumor effects of PD-1 inhibitors while showing low toxicity

Ligand-L can specifically target cancer cells with elevated copper levels, triggering the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which subsequently induce DNA damage, apoptosis, and cell death, thereby exerting its anticancer effects

sec.p: Brewer and Merajver first described the anticancer effect of TTM in 1999. TTM reduces angiogenesis by inhibiting the activity of SOD1, thereby exerting its antitumor effects.110 Nan et al. demonstrated in animal models that the combination therapy of TTM with lenvatinib significantly reduced angiogenesis in liver tumors, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy.102 Recent studies have shown that TTM inhibits the activity of mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase 1/2 (MAP2K1/2) by lowering copper levels, thereby suppressing the growth of papillary thyroid cancer and colon cancer.111 Notably, TTM also enhances the antitumor activity of MAP2K1/2 and BRAFV600E inhibitors, such as sorafenib and vemurafenib. Trientine is another copper chelator primarily used in the treatment of Wilson's disease but has also been investigated for its potential in oncology. Trientine works by forming a stable complex with copper ions, which is then excreted through the urine, thereby reducing copper levels within the body.112 The Moriguchi team discovered a novel mechanism by which trientine acts as a potential anti-angiogenic drug. It achieves this by chelating copper ions to reduce IL-8 production. This effect significantly inhibits tumor growth, enhances the apoptotic potential of tumor cells, and effectively reduces tumor microvascular density.105 Shi and colleagues synthesized a novel copper chelator, JYFY-001. Their study showed that JYFY-001 not only strongly inhibits the proliferation of colorectal cancer cells and induces apoptosis but also enhances the antitumor efficacy of PD-1 inhibitors. Furthermore, JYFY-001 demonstrates low toxicity, highlighting its potential as both a targeted therapeutic agent and a synergistic partner in immunotherapy.108

While copper chelators show great potential in inducing cuproptosis and enhancing cancer treatment, several challenges must be addressed. Copper is an essential nutrient for normal cellular functions, and indiscriminate copper chelation could lead to systemic toxicity. Therefore, developing copper chelators that selectively target cancer cells without affecting normal tissues is crucial. Furthermore, optimal dosing and combination strategies need to be established. Copper chelation alone may not be sufficient to eradicate tumors, but combining copper chelators with other therapies, such as chemotherapy, radiation, or immunotherapy, may offer synergistic effects and improve therapeutic outcomes.

sec.title: Copper ionophores
sec.p: Copper ionophores are compounds that enhance the intracellular accumulation of copper ions by facilitating their transport across the cell membrane. These agents increase intracellular copper concentrations, potentially overwhelming copper-dependent cellular systems and inducing cuproptosis. In cancer therapy, the role of copper ionophores is particularly relevant, as they can selectively target rapidly dividing tumor cells that typically exhibit altered copper metabolism. Some copper ionophores, such as elesclomol (ES) and disulfiram (DSF), have shown potential anticancer effects (Table 2).

caption.p: Different copper ionophores for digestive system cancer treatment.

tr.td: Elesclomol induces copper-dependent ferroptosis in CRC cells via degradation of ATP7A
Elesclomol induced cuproptosis is enhanced by ferroptosis inducers erastin and sorafenib in liver cancer
Disulfiram inhibits the proliferation of gastric cancer cells by regulating the key protein MMP-2 in the WNT and NF-κB signaling pathways
Disulfiram can enhance the efficacy of 5-fluorouracil against colorectal cancer, particularly by inhibiting NF-κB activity to reverse drug resistance
The combined use of disulfiram and H2S can inhibit the growth of liver cancer cells and tumor development by targeting ALDH activity
Disulfiram reduces the survival and angiogenic capabilities of tumor cells under hypoxic conditions by inhibiting the expression and activity of HIF-2α
The combined use of disulfiram with chemoimmunotherapy can activate the cGAS–STING signaling pathway by inhibiting PARP1
DSF/Cu inhibits the proliferation of colorectal cancer by inducing autophagy through targeting ULK1, rather than apoptosis

Disulfiram undergoes metabolic conversion to diethyldithiocarbamate, and subsequently forms a complex with copper, known as the CuET. CuET inhibits the progression of colorectal cancer by regulating the miR-16-5p and miR-15b-5p/ALDH1A3/PKM2 axis-mediated aerobic glycolysis pathway
Disulfram reduces intracellular H₂S levels and inhibits the proliferation of HCT116 cells through its metabolite CuDDC
DSF/Cu can suppress the growth of colorectal cancer by enhancing the expression of immunogenic cell death molecules.

Acidic pH enhances the toxicity of the disulfram-Cu complex in breast and colon cancer cells. This effect is associated with changes in cell metabolism, altered AKT kinase and NF-κB activity, and increased ROS production
Disulfiram/copper complex inhibits NF-κB activity and potentiates the cytotoxic effect of gemcitabine on colon and breast cancer cell lines
Inhibition of NRF2 enhances the sensitivity of hepatocellular carcinoma to DSF-induced ferroptosis and augments the synergistic antitumor effect with sorafenib

The therapeutic effect of CD47 blockade is enhanced by DSF/Cu through the activation of innate immune responses. This enhancement is achieved by inducing immunogenic cell death via the inhibition of the ubiquitin-proteasome system, which is mediated by NPL4

DSF/Cu upregulates PD-L1 expression by inhibiting PARP1 and activating GSK3β phosphorylation, leading to immune evasion in liver cancer, and its combination with anti-PD-1 antibody significantly improves therapeutic efficacy
DSF/Cu effectively inhibits the metastasis and epithelial-mesenchymal transition of hepatocellular carcinoma by suppressing the NF-κB and TGF-β pathways
By inhibiting the NF-kB signaling pathway, DSF, when combined with the standard chemoradiation regimen, can effectively target both stem and non-stem cells in pancreatic cancer
SIH facilitates the transport and intracellular release of Cu2+ in HepG2 cells, thereby triggering mitochondria-mediated apoptosis

Curcumin can regulate the metabolic processes of lipids, RNA, NADH, and NADPH in colorectal cancer cells, and upregulate positive cuproptosis mediators, thereby potentially promoting cancer cell death

fn.p: ICC: intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma. BC: breast cancer.

sec.p: Elesclomol binds to copper ions, facilitating their transport across the cell membrane and into the mitochondria. This copper overload leads to the inhibition of mitochondrial respiration, as copper displaces other metal ions critical for electron transport.133 This disruption of mitochondrial function increases ROS production, which damages cellular structures. Oxidative stress triggers cell death pathways, resulting in cuproptosis. Elesclomol has not yet been shown to have effective therapeutic outcomes in clinical trials. A phase III trial results revealed that although the combination of elesclomol with paclitaxel did not improve progression-free survival in melanoma patients, elesclomol exhibited better anti-tumor effects in patients with low lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels.134 Emerging evidence suggests that intracellular copper accumulation could also promote ferroptosis. Elesclomol downregulates the expression of ATP7A, causing Cu2+ to be retained inside cells and subsequently accumulate ROS, eventually leading to SLC7A11 degradation and ferroptosis in CRC cells.113 Similarly, Wang et al. found that the ferroptosis inducers sorafenib and erastin could enhance the cuproptosis induced by elesclomol, offering a new perspective for liver cancer treatment.114

Originally used as a drug to treat alcoholism, disulfiram has been found to have anticancer properties through its action on copper homeostasis. Disulfram interacts with copper to form the metabolite bis(diethyldithiocarbamate)-Cu (CuET), which transports Cu ions across the cell membrane. The elevated copper levels lead to the generation of ROS, causing oxidative damage to cellular components. Moreover, the anticancer mechanisms of disulfram treatment include anti-proliferation, induction of tumor cell death, reversal of drug resistance, and immunomodulatory effects. Zhang and colleagues demonstrated that disulfiram significantly inhibits the proliferation of gastric cancer cells in vitro and exerts its effects by modulating the key protein matrix metallopeptidase 2 (MMP-2) within the WNT and NF-κB signaling pathways.115 Skrott et al. discovered that disulfram exerts its effects through its metabolite CuET, which specifically targets the p97 segregase adapter NPL4. This targeting inhibits the p97-dependent protein degradation pathway, leading to abnormal protein aggregation in tumor cells and ultimately resulting in cell death.135 Additionally, studies have shown that the combination of disulfiram and 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) exhibits significant cytotoxic effects on human colorectal cancer cell lines.
