DSF acts as a potential chemosensitizing agent, particularly in reversing 5-FU resistance.116 Huang et al. revealed that DSF activates the cGAS‒STING signaling pathway by inhibiting poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase 1 (PARP1), leading to the upregulation of cytokines. This process significantly enhances the infiltration of CD8+ T cells into tumors, playing a crucial role in activating anti-tumor immunity.119 Given the pivotal role of copper in the pathogenesis of gastrointestinal tumors, there has been a surge of interest in recent years in developing therapeutic strategies based on copper ionophores. Numerous clinical trials on gastrointestinal tumors have utilized copper ionophores, as detailed in Table 3.

tr.td: Refractory solid tumors or metastatic pancreatic cancer
Nab-paclitaxel plus gemcitabin or FOLFIRINOX or single-agent gemcitabine
Refractory malignancies that have metastasized to the liver

sec.title: Small compounds
sec.p: Small compounds that disrupt copper homeostasis have the potential to induce or enhance the sensitivity of cancer cells to cuproptosis. Recent studies have identified several such small compounds that can disturb the intracellular copper balance, thereby inducing or potentiating cuproptosis, offering promising new avenues for cancer therapy (Table 4). The Yang group reported the potential role and mechanism of 4-octyl itaconate (4-OI) in the treatment of colorectal cancer. They found that 4-OI could promote cuproptosis by inhibiting the key enzyme glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) in aerobic glycolysis, thereby inhibiting the proliferation of colorectal cancer cells.136 Similarly, the study also has shown that zinc pyrithione (ZnPT) disrupts copper homeostasis and promotes the oligomerization of DLAT, significantly inhibiting the proliferation of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) cells and inducing cuproptosis.137 Moreover, the ferroptosis inducers sorafenib and erastin have been shown to enhance the effect of copper ionophores in inducing cuproptosis in HCC cells. First, they inhibit the degradation of FDX1 by mitochondrial matrix-associated proteases, thereby upregulating protein lipoylation levels. Second, these inducers suppress cystine uptake, which in turn reduces intracellular GSH synthesis. This study reveals a cross-talk between cuproptosis and ferroptosis, suggesting that the combined use of copper ionophores and ferroptosis inducers to target both ferroptosis and cuproptosis may provide a novel approach for cancer treatment.114

caption.p: Different small compounds for digestive system cancer treatment.

tr.td: 4-octyl itaconate inhibits aerobic glycolysis by targeting GAPDH and promotes cuproptosis
Sorafenib can promote cuproptosis by enhancing protein lipoylation and reducing intracellular glutathione levels
Erastin can promote cuproptosis by enhancing protein lipoylation and reducing intracellular glutathione levels
Curcumin may play a significant role in anti-hepatocellular carcinoma by regulating ferroptosis and cuproptosis, but the specific mechanisms were not elaborated in the article

sec.title: Nanoparticles
sec.p: Nanomedicine, which involves the use of nanoscale materials for medical applications, has gained considerable attention in oncology due to its potential to overcome the limitations of traditional cancer therapies. These limitations often include issues like poor bioavailability, non-selectivity, and the development of drug resistance. Nanoparticles are exceptionally well-suited for targeted drug delivery systems due to their distinctive characteristics (Table 5). These include the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, which allows for better accumulation in tumor tissues, the capacity for surface functionalization to improve stability and biocompatibility, and the ability to conjugate with targeting ligands that enable precise delivery to cancer cells. In addition, the TME is a complex and dynamic ecosystem composed of various cellular and non-cellular components, characterized by mild acidity, hypoxia, elevated levels of the reductant GSH, and increased ROS levels, which distinguish it from normal tissues. By engineering nanoparticles that respond to the specific characteristics of the TME, researchers can enhance drug delivery, improve therapeutic efficacy, and overcome resistance mechanisms associated with the TME. Jin et al. developed a cuproptosis-mediated immunotherapeutic nanoparticle reactor (CCJD–FA), which disassembles within tumor cells to release CaO₂ and Cu²⁺, generating H₂O₂ and producing O₂ through a Fenton-like reaction, thus alleviating hypoxia and inducing cuproptosis.139 Additionally, CCJD-FA significantly reduces the intracellular levels of GSH, decreases lactate production, inhibits cellular glycolysis, and lowers ATP generation. This leads to blockade of the ATP7B protein, increasing intracellular copper accumulation and ultimately inducing cuproptosis in tumor cells. Moreover, CCJD-FA inhibits interferon-gamma-induced PD-L1 expression, disrupting immune tolerance and enhancing the anti-tumor immune response. When used in combination with PD-1 antibodies, CCJD-FA further enhances the inhibition of primary tumors as well as distant, untreated tumors, and activates a systemic anti-tumor immune response. Similarly, Wu et al. designed a tumor-specific “nontoxicity-to-toxicity” transition strategy using copper-doped hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles as functional carriers. These nanoparticles rapidly release Cu²⁺ under mildly acidic conditions of the tumor microenvironment, accelerating the release of DSF and forming toxic CuET complexes and ROS within tumor cells, thereby significantly enhancing the anticancer efficacy of DSF.140

caption.p: Different nanosystems for digestive system cancer treatment.

tr.td: CaO2; Cu2+; DTPH; DSPE-PEG-FA; and JQ-1

CCJD-FA releases Cu2+ and CaO2 within tumor cells, inhibiting intracellular glycolysis and ATP production, thereby inducing cuproptosis and alleviating hypoxia. Additionally, CCJD-FA promotes the secretion of IFN-γ in tumor tissues while suppressing IFN-γ-induced PD-L1 expression, thereby enhancing the antitumor immune response
HPB; Au–Pt nanozymes; Cu; and cancer cell membrane

Cu/APH-M alleviates hypoxia in the tumor microenvironment by decomposing hydrogen peroxide to produce oxygen and delivers copper ions to induce cuproptosis. Additionally, it upregulates immune-related genes, activates antitumor immune responses, and reverses the upregulation of PD-L1 caused by radiotherapy
E. coli@Cu2O nanohybrid that successfully reversed immunosuppression in colon cancer through NIR II photothermal-enhanced ferroptosis and cuproptosis, activating antitumor immune responses
Cu2+; elesclomol; sodium alginate; and galactose

Elesclomol–Cu–Alg hydrogel induced DLAT oligomerization and cuproptosis in colorectal cancer cells, which reduced the expression of PD-L1 and DNA damage repair proteins, thereby enhancing the tumor's sensitivity to radiation therapy and immunotherapy
Cu2(PO4)(OH)NPs can transform into ultrasmall Cu9S8 NPs in H2S-rich environments, inducing cuproptosis and pyroptosis by increasing intracellular ROS levels and copper ion release

CMCO nanozymes have multiple enzyme-mimicking activities, capable of inducing high-efficiency ferroptosis-enhanced cuproptosis through mild photothermal effects, thereby effectively inhibiting the growth of colorectal cancer cells
Dendritic mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles(DMONs); Cu; and phloretin

CDPh achieves efficient antitumor effects by in-situ synthesizing Cu Single-atom nanozyme(SAzyme) in tumor cells, disrupting copper metabolism through GSH consumption and copper ion release, inducing cuproptosis, and enhancing Cu SAzyme activity via photothermal effects to promote ROS generation, leading to metabolic disorder and energy depletion
NAC; 4-mercaptobenzoic acid;HAuCl4; NaOH; NaBH4; and CuCl2

Au25(NAMB)18NCs-Cu2+@SA/NHGs enhances PTT and PDT through near-infrared light response, while regulating TME to promote cuproptosis. It also consumes excessive GSH to form Cu+, generating ·OH to kill tumor cells, thereby achieving GSH consumption-enhanced PDT and CDT

CuO2/DDP@SiO2 nanoparticles generate oxygen and raise pH in acidic environments, alleviating hypoxia and acidosis in the tumor microenvironment, and enhancing the effects of cuproptosis, chemotherapy, and chemodynamic therapy. They also deplete glutathione, reducing its binding with cisplatin, thereby improving the chemotherapeutic effect of cisplatin

O2-PFH@CHPI NPs deplete glutathione in tumor microenvironments, leading to ROS accumulation. Under near-infrared light, they accelerate catalysis and oxygen release, enhancing photodynamic therapy and copper death. Additionally, redox imbalance promotes lipid peroxidation and GPX4 inactivation, boosting ferroptosis

CuET@PH NPs inhibit oxidative phosphorylation under hypoxia and glycolysis under hyperbaric oxygen. HBO shifts CSCs' metabolism from glycolysis to oxidative phosphorylation, enhancing the effects of CuET@PH NPs

sec.p: Utilizing light-responsive nanosystems to induce cuproptosis in tumor tissues represents a highly promising therapeutic strategy. The properties of the light source, such as selection, intensity, phase, and amplitude, can all be precisely controlled. This photo-controlled activation approach is an ideal tool for inducing skin sagging. Using light-responsive nanosystems to induce cuproptosis in tumor tissues represents a highly promising therapeutic strategy. The Yang group successfully developed a novel Au nanocluster-Cu2+@sodium alginate/hyaluronic acid nanohybrid gel. This gel not only possesses excellent biocompatibility, stability, and targeting ability but also responds to near-infrared light, thereby synergistically enhancing photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT).147 By regulating the TME, the gel promotes cuproptosis. Additionally, the gel can consume excessive GSH in tumor cells, forming Cu+ and producing hydroxyl radicals to kill tumor cells, thereby achieving GSH consumption-enhanced PDT and chemodynamic therapy (CDT). Likewise, Ruan et al. reported the development of an engineered microbial nanohybrid based on Escherichia coli (E. coli) and copper oxide (Cu2O) nanoparticles for specific colon cancer therapies.142 This hybrid can convert endogenous hydrogen sulfide (H2S) in the tumor microenvironment into CuxS and generate photothermal effects under the NIR II biological window, thereby enhancing ferroptosis and cuproptosis while activating antitumor immune responses. Nanoparticle drugs not only activate antitumor immune responses but also enhance the effects of radiotherapy. The ES-Cu-Alg hydrogel induces the oligomerization of DLAT and cuproptosis in colorectal cancer cells, thereby reducing PD-L1 expression and enhancing the effectiveness of immunotherapy. Furthermore, in tumor-bearing mouse models, researchers found that the hydrogel significantly increased the tumor's sensitivity to radiotherapy and extended the survival of tumor-bearing mice.143

However, despite the promising potential of nanomedicines in cuproptosis-based cancer treatment, several challenges remain. One major limitation is the potential toxicity of copper-loaded nanoparticles. While copper is essential for normal cellular function, excessive copper accumulation can lead to systemic toxicity, posing risks to normal tissues and organs. Ensuring targeted delivery and minimizing off-target effects are critical for maximizing the therapeutic window and minimizing adverse effects. Additionally, the long-term stability, biocompatibility, and clearance of nanoparticles are still major concerns. Efficient drug release and controlled biodegradation of nanomaterials remain areas of intense research to optimize therapeutic outcomes. As research continues, the development of more advanced nanocarriers and delivery systems will likely pave the way for the successful clinical application of cuproptosis-targeted therapies in cancer treatment.

sec.title: Conclusion and perspectives
sec.p: This review provides a comprehensive exploration of the role of copper metabolism and cuproptosis in digestive system cancers, highlighting the dual nature of copper as both a promoter of tumor progression and a potential therapeutic target. This review then examines the implications of cuproptosis in various digestive system cancers, discussing how cuproptosis-related genes and pathways influence tumor progression, immune responses, and therapeutic outcomes. We highlight the critical role of copper in cancer biology and the potential of cuproptosis as a novel therapeutic target.

Copper chelators and ionophores hold significant potential for inducing cuproptosis and thereby enhancing the efficacy of cancer treatments. Despite these advances, significant challenges remain in translating cuproptosis-based therapies into clinical practice. One major limitation is the lack of tumor specificity in current copper-modulating agents, which can lead to systemic toxicity and off-target effects. Future research should prioritize the development of more selective copper-targeting agents and the optimization of combination therapies, with the aim of maximizing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing adverse effects. Meanwhile, developing targeted delivery systems, such as nanoparticles responsive to the tumor microenvironment or ligand-conjugated carriers, is crucial to minimize these risks. Additionally, resistance mechanisms, such as the upregulation of copper efflux proteins (e.g., ATP7A/B) or metabolic adaptations in cancer cells, pose a significant barrier to the efficacy of cuproptosis-inducing therapies. Understanding these resistance mechanisms and identifying predictive biomarkers for patient stratification are essential for designing effective combination therapies. While preclinical studies have shown promising results, the transition to clinical trials faces challenges such as optimal dosing, long-term safety, and patient selection. Multicenter trials are necessary to evaluate the efficacy of cuproptosis-based therapies across diverse cancer types and stages.

In conclusion, the discovery of cuproptosis has opened new horizons in the treatment of digestive system cancers, offering a unique approach to target metabolic vulnerabilities in tumor cells. While significant progress has been made, further research is needed to refine therapeutic strategies, develop predictive biomarkers, and optimize delivery systems. By addressing these challenges through innovative research and interdisciplinary collaboration, we can unlock the full potential of cuproptosis as a powerful tool in the fight against cancer.
